text:
Team GB’s George Mills was furious with his French rival Hugo Hay after a fall in their Olympic 5000m qualifier on Wednesday morning.

On the final straight of the first heat at the Stade de France, Mills was at the centre of a collision with Hay, which also brought down three other athletes.

Mills, the son of formerLeeds Unitedand England defender Danny Mills, was livid as he walked across the finish line and immediately confronted Hay.

The Frenchman, who finished seventh, attempted to diffuse the situation as Mills approached and pointed a finger in his face.

The pair exchanged words but Mills did not appear to be satisfied with Hay’s response as he brushed the Frenchman’s hand off his shoulder as he walked away.

Earlier in the race, Canada’s Mohamed Ahmed had also fallen in a tightly packed field.

But the major flashpoint came in the final 100 metres of the race when Mills attempted to run beside Hay but was blocked by the Frenchman.

Mills quickly lost balance and tumbled to the track, taking the Netherlands’ Mike Foppen, Spain’s Thierry Ndikumwenayo and the Refugee Team’s Dominic Lokinyomo Lobalu down with him.

‘I think it’s pretty clear, I got stepped out on as I was about to kick in the home straight then, boom, the French lad took me down,’ Mills said after the race.

Asked what he told Hay, Mills replied: ‘Probably not allowed to say.’

Asked if he will appeal, Mills said: ‘I haven’t seen anyone but I’m pretty sure BA will put in an appeal, I hope.

‘From my perspective that was the perfect qualifier for me going through the first 2k in six minutes, I was like, ‘alright, nobody in this field can run away from me in this pace’.

‘So I was just sitting and biding my time, gonna to kick off the home straight and bang, hit the deck. What can you do?’

After the race, it was confirmed that Mills has now advanced to the next round following a decision from the race referee.

The three others fallers – Foppen, Ndikumwenayo and Lobalu have also been put through.

When asked for his version of the collision with Mills, Hay said: ‘I don’t know what happened. I didn’t feel anything. I was surprised that George came to see me after the race, because I didn’t feel anything, well no more than usual.

‘It pushed in the last 400m, it was really a massacre. I turned around, there was no one left. It was incredible.

‘I have no idea what’s going to happen, it scares me. For me, it happened clearly. That’s what I said to George. If I’m guilty, sorry, but I think I was pushed. I don’t think I did anything wrong, but it scares me a little bit.

‘I hope it will work out and that I will be able to get into the final. If ever the protest passes and I’m eliminated, of course, it will hurt but I know that I had the level to qualify and that I have never been so strong. For me, I am qualified, that’s the most important thing.’

Paula Radcliffe, who was covering the race for the BBC, said after the race: ‘There’s a lot to look back at, I lot to tidy up and decide whose fault it was.

‘If you decide to jog that slightly, it’s kind of your own fault. You end up with all of those falls.’

BBC pundit Steve Cram added: ‘Paula’s right to some extent, with the exception of Nordas most have got themselves to blame.

‘George was in a bad position from a long way out and should have checked out much earlier.’

Former Great Britain 400m runner, Allison Curbishley, said on BBC Radio 5 Live: ‘George Mills has done this so many times, he doesn’t put himself in the best of positions.

‘You are always going to get yourself caught up when when there’s too many people.’
